description.summary: The Adze is a vampiric spirit of Ewe folklore (Ghana and Togo) that travels at night in the form of a firefly, drinking blood — especially from children — and causing illness; if caught, it transforms into human shape and is regarded as the spirit possessing a witch.
description.full: The Adze is a vampiric being from the folklore of the Ewe people of southeastern Ghana and southern Togo in West Africa. In its natural state it takes the form of a firefly, slipping through keyholes, cracks in walls, and under closed doors at night to feed on the blood of sleeping people, especially children. If caught, the Adze loses its insect form and reverts to a human appearance. People — male or female — believed to be possessed by an Adze are termed witches (Ewe: 'abasom') and are blamed for misfortune in the community: an old person whose children are dying while the elder remains healthy, the brother of a more successful sister, or a poor person envious of the rich. There is, in tradition, no defense against an Adze. Modern folklorists generally read the Adze tradition as an explanatory framework for malaria and other insect-borne disease in a region where mosquitoes and similar vectors caused otherwise unaccountable nighttime deaths.

etymology.origin: Ewe (a Gbe language of Ghana and Togo).
etymology.meaning: Refers specifically to the vampiric spirit; the same English word adze is also used for the unrelated woodworking tool, but the two are not etymologically connected.
etymology.first_use: Documented in 19th- and 20th-century European ethnographic and missionary literature on the Ewe; brought to wider English-language attention through 20th-century vampire and African-folklore compendia.

related_phenomena.name: Asanbosam
related_phenomena.description: Ashanti vampiric being of West African folklore with iron hooks for feet

related_phenomena.name: Obayifo
related_phenomena.description: Ashanti witch-spirit said to fly as a luminous body at night and drink blood

related_phenomena.name: Soucouyant
related_phenomena.description: Caribbean diasporic vampire-witch with West African folkloric roots

features.description: Travels in the form of a firefly (a small, glowing insect) at night.
Reverts to human appearance if caught; this is when it is identified as a possessing witch-spirit.

physical.morphology: Shape-shifting between insect (firefly) and human forms; in human form often associated with a particular individual believed to be possessed.

abilities.description: Feeds on the blood of sleeping people, especially children, causing wasting illness and death.
Assumes the body of a firefly to enter homes through small openings.
When in human form, may possess people, who are then identified as witches (abasom) by the community.
Held responsible for disease, child mortality, and bad luck in households where envy or family tension is suspected.

behavior.diet: Blood (especially of sleeping children); also said to consume coconut water, palm oil, and human flesh in some accounts
patterns: Enters homes through keyholes, cracks, and gaps under doors
Targets the families of those toward whom the host harbours envy
Difficult or impossible to defend against once a possession is suspected

range.description: The Ewe-speaking area of West Africa, comprising southeastern Ghana (Volta Region) and southern Togo, with related traditions among neighbouring Gbe-speaking peoples.

first_recorded.source: Ewe oral tradition; recorded in 19th-century European missionary and ethnographic accounts

peak_activity_periods.description: Period in which Adze beliefs were most widely recorded by European observers and African scholars

origins.cultural_roots: Ewe-speaking peoples of southeastern Ghana and southern Togo. Archaeological and historical evidence places Ewe settlement of the West African coastal region by around the 13th century.
origins.earliest_accounts: Recorded in 19th- and 20th-century European-language ethnography of the Ewe; popularized in English-language vampire literature in the late 20th century.
origins.folklore_connections: Connected to a wider West African belief complex of nocturnal flying witches and blood-drinking spirits that includes the Ashanti obayifo and asanbosam.

timeline.event: Ewe settlement of the coastal West African region (modern Ghana and Togo) per archaeological reconstruction
timeline.significance: Cultural baseline in which Adze tradition develops
timeline.source: Wikipedia: Adze (folklore), citing standard Ewe historiography

timeline.event: Matthew Bunson includes the Adze in The Vampire Encyclopedia
timeline.significance: Brings the tradition to a broad English-language audience

history.modern_era: The Adze remains an active part of Ewe folk discourse around illness, witchcraft, and child mortality, even as it has been adopted by Western popular culture as a stock 'African vampire'. Folklorists generally interpret the figure as an etiological framework for insect-borne disease.

documentary.name: The Vampire Encyclopedia entry on the Adze
documentary.description: Matthew Bunson's encyclopedic entry summarising Ewe Adze beliefs.
documentary.analysis: A widely cited tertiary reference; not a field ethnography but a synthesis from earlier sources.
documentary.source: Bunson, M. (1993), The Vampire Encyclopedia, Thames & Hudson

paranormal.name: Witch-possession explanation
paranormal.description: Within Ewe tradition, the Adze is a real spirit that can possess humans, accounting for misfortune, disease, and antisocial behaviour in a community.
evidence_cited: Ethnographic records of accusations against suspected hosts

scientific.name: Disease etiology
scientific.description: Folklorists and historians interpret the Adze as a culturally specific framework for explaining and responding to malaria and other insect-borne diseases endemic to coastal West Africa, where mosquitoes and other vectors cause sudden, otherwise unexplained childhood deaths.
proponents: Folklorists summarised in Wikipedia entry; Atlas Obscura coverage

skeptical.name: Witchcraft accusation pattern
skeptical.description: Anthropologists note that Adze accusations follow standard witchcraft-accusation patterns — falling on the elderly, the marginal, the envious, and outsiders — rather than on physical evidence.
evidence_cited: Demographic profile of accused: elderly, women with successful brothers, the poor

traditions: Ewe tradition of nocturnal vampiric witch-spirits
folklore.beliefs: Belief in Adze possession is part of a broader Ewe witchcraft framework in which envy, family tension, and unexplained illness are explained through spiritual possession.

indigenous.significance: Adze beliefs are an active part of Ewe spiritual life, not merely a relic; they continue to shape responses to illness, death, and social tension.
indigenous.sensitivity_note: Western 'African vampire' framings often reduce the Adze to a stock monster; Ewe traditions integrate it into a wider system of community ethics, witchcraft, and healing.
indigenous.respect_guidelines: Cite Ewe-language terminology accurately, distinguish folk belief from sensationalised media accounts, and avoid using Adze beliefs to caricature the cultures that maintain them.

games: Featured in various Western 'world monsters' bestiaries and tabletop games
other: Atlas Obscura, 'In West Africa, the Adze Is an Insectoid Source of Misfortune' (Emma Gross, 2020)

researchers.name: Matthew Bunson
researchers.contribution: Author of The Vampire Encyclopedia (1993), a key English-language reference for the Adze

research.ongoing_studies: No active cryptozoological investigation; ongoing study sits within the anthropology of West African religion and witchcraft.

sources.title: In West Africa, the Adze Is an Insectoid Source of Misfortune
